# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark results, ablation findings, cost/latency measurements, a concrete context-collapse incident, and method-design details for ACE as a context adaptation framework for agent and finance-oriented LLM tasks.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Agentic Context Engineering, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Across the reported benchmarks, ACE is described as outperforming strong baselines by +10.6% on agents and +8.6% on finance.

## Source Excerpt

"Across agent and domain-specific benchmarks, ACE optimizes contexts both offline (e.g., system prompts) and online (e.g., agent memory), consistently outperforming strong baselines: +10.6% on agents and +8.6% on finance"

## Note

These are aggregate improvement figures stated in the abstract rather than a full table row.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: result
citation: Zhang et al., Agentic Context Engineering, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ACE is reported to adapt effectively without labeled supervision by using natural execution feedback.

## Source Excerpt

"Notably, ACE could adapt effectively without labeled supervision and instead by leveraging natural execution feedback."

## Note

This states the feedback source used for adaptation, but does not fully establish when such unlabeled feedback is sufficient across all domains.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: result
citation: Zhang et al., Agentic Context Engineering, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On the AppWorld leaderboard, ACE is reported to match the top-ranked production-level agent on overall average while using a smaller open-source model.

## Source Excerpt

"On the AppWorld leaderboard, ACE matches the top-ranked production-level agent on the overall average and surpasses it on the harder test-challenge split, despite using a smaller open-source model."

## Note

The benchmark comparison is specifically to the leaderboard snapshot cited in the paper.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On the harder AppWorld test-challenge split, online ReAct + ACE is reported to surpass IBM CUGA by 8.4% in TGC and 0.7% in SGC.

## Source Excerpt

"With online adaptation, ReAct + ACE even surpasses IBM CUGA by 8.4% in TGC and 0.7% in SGC on the harder test-challenge split"

## Note

The cited comparison is limited to the harder test-challenge split rather than all reported splits.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: incident
citation: Zhang et al., Section 2.2 Context Collapse
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
In the AppWorld case study, a rewritten context dropped from 18,282 tokens at step 60 to 122 tokens at the next step, while accuracy fell from 66.7 to 57.1, below the 63.7 baseline without adaptation.

## Source Excerpt

"For instance, at step 60 the context contained 18,282 tokens and achieved an accuracy of 66.7, but at the very next step it collapsed to just 122 tokens, with accuracy dropping to 57.1—worse than the baseline accuracy of 63.7 without adaptation."

## Note

This is presented as an observed case study of context collapse and supports the claimed risk of monolithic rewriting rather than a universal failure rate.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Zhang et al., Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ACE divides work across three roles: Generator, Reflector, and Curator.

## Source Excerpt

"ACE introduces a structured division of labor across three roles (Figure 4): the Generator, which produces reasoning trajectories; the Reflector, which distills concrete insights from successes and errors; and the Curator, which integrates these insights into structured context updates."

## Note

This is a method-design statement, not a benchmark outcome.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: method
citation: Zhang et al., Section 3.1 Incremental Delta Updates
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ACE represents context as itemized bullets that contain metadata and content rather than as one monolithic prompt.

## Source Excerpt

"A core design principle of ACE is to represent context as a collection of structured, itemized bullets, rather than a single monolithic prompt... [a bullet] consists of (1) metadata, including a unique identifier and counters tracking how often it was marked helpful or harmful; and (2) content, capturing a small unit such as a reusable strategy, domain concept, or common failure mode."

## Note

This describes the internal structure used for adaptation.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: method
citation: Zhang et al., Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ACE merges compact delta entries into existing context with lightweight non-LLM logic, and multiple deltas can be merged in parallel.

## Source Excerpt

"The Curator then synthesizes these lessons into compact delta entries, which are merged deterministically into the existing context by lightweight, non-LLM logic. Because updates are itemized and localized, multiple deltas can be merged in parallel, enabling batched adaptation at scale."

## Note

This is the paper's stated mechanism for scaling updates and avoiding full rewrites.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On AppWorld offline adaptation with ground-truth labels, ReAct + ACE reached 59.4 average accuracy versus 46.4 for ReAct + GEPA, 46.0 for ReAct + ICL, and 42.4 for ReAct baseline.

## Source Excerpt

"ReAct 63.7 42.9 41.5 21.6 42.4" and "ReAct + GEPA ✓ ... 46.4 + 4.0" and "ReAct + ICL ✓ ... 46.0 + 3.6" and "ReAct + ACE ✓ ... 59.4 + 17.0"

## Note

The average metric is the rightmost AppWorld average reported in Table 1.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On AppWorld offline adaptation without ground-truth labels, ReAct + ACE reached 57.2 average accuracy, which the table reports as +14.8 over the 42.4 ReAct baseline.

## Source Excerpt

"ReAct + ACE ✗ ... 57.2 + 14.8" and "ReAct 63.7 42.9 41.5 21.6 42.4"

## Note

This evidence is specific to the no-GT-label offline AppWorld condition.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On AppWorld online adaptation without ground-truth labels, ReAct + ACE reached 59.5 average accuracy versus 51.9 for Dynamic Cheatsheet cumulative mode and 42.4 for the ReAct baseline.

## Source Excerpt

"ReAct + DC (CU) ✗ ... 51.9 + 9.5" and "ReAct + ACE ✗ ... 59.5 + 17.1" and "ReAct 63.7 42.9 41.5 21.6 42.4"

## Note

The paper also summarizes this as ACE outperforming prior adaptive methods such as Dynamic Cheatsheet in the online setting.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On the financial benchmarks with ground-truth labels in offline adaptation, ACE reached 78.3 on FiNER and 85.5 on Formula, for an 81.9 average.

## Source Excerpt

"ACE ✓ 78.3 + 7.6 ... 85.5 + 18.0 ... 81.9 + 12.8"

## Note

These values are reported in the offline adaptation section of Table 2.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In the same offline financial setting with ground-truth labels, ACE's 81.9 average exceeded GEPA's 72.5, MIPROv2's 70.9, ICL's 69.6, and the base LLM's 69.1.

## Source Excerpt

"Base LLM 70.7 67.5 69.1" and "ICL ✓ ... 69.6 + 0.5" and "MIPROv2 ✓ ... 70.9 + 1.8" and "GEPA ✓ ... 72.5 + 3.4" and "ACE ✓ ... 81.9 + 12.8"

## Note

This comparison is restricted to the financial offline adaptation results shown in Table 2.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: claim-support
citation: Zhang et al., Table 2 and Section 4.4 Analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper reports that when reliable feedback signals are absent, adaptive methods can degrade; in Table 2, online ACE without GT labels scored 72.9 average versus 76.6 with GT labels, and DC without GT labels scored 65.4 versus 71.8 with GT labels.

## Source Excerpt

"However, we also observe that in the absence of reliable feedback signals (e.g., ground-truth labels or execution outcomes), both ACE and other adaptive methods such as Dynamic Cheatsheet may degrade" and "DC (CU) ✓ ... 71.8 + 2.7" / "DC (CU) ✗ ... 65.4 − 3.7" / "ACE ✓ ... 76.6 + 7.5" / "ACE ✗ ... 72.9 + 3.8"

## Note

This is a limitation claim tied to the quality of feedback, and the reported degradation depends on the specific benchmark/task setting.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Table 4(a)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
For offline AppWorld adaptation, ACE is reported at 9,517 seconds latency and 357 rollouts versus GEPA at 53,898 seconds latency and 1,434 rollouts.

## Source Excerpt

"ReAct + GEPA 53898 1434" and "ReAct + ACE 9517 (-82.3%) 357 (-75.1%)"

## Note

These cost/speed figures come from Table 4(a), which covers offline AppWorld adaptation.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Table 4(b)
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
For online FiNER adaptation, ACE is reported at 5,503 seconds latency and $2.9 token cost versus Dynamic Cheatsheet at 65,104 seconds latency and $17.7 token cost.

## Source Excerpt

"DC (CU) 65104 17.7" and "ACE 5503 (-91.5%) 2.9 (-83.6%)"

## Note

These figures are specific to the online FiNER cost comparison in Table 4(b).
---

## Summary

version: evidence.v1

evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Zhang et al., Agentic Context Engineering, Abstract

Across the reported benchmarks, ACE is described as outperforming strong baselines by +10.6% on agents and +8.6% on finance.

"Across agent and domain-specific benchmarks, ACE optimizes contexts both offline (e.g., system prompts) and online (e.g., agent memory), consistently outperforming strong baselines: +10.6% on agents and +8.6% on finance"

On the harder AppWorld test-challenge split, online ReAct + ACE is reported to surpass IBM CUGA by 8.4% in TGC and 0.7% in SGC.

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: incident
citation: Zhang et al., Section 2.2 Context Collapse
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
In the AppWorld case study, a rewritten context dropped from 18,282 tokens at step 60 to 122 tokens at the next step, while accuracy fell from 66.7 to 57.1, below the 63.7 baseline without adaptation.

## Source Excerpt

"For instance, at step 60 the context contained 18,282 tokens and achieved an accuracy of 66.7, but at the very next step it collapsed to just 122 tokens, with accuracy dropping to 57.1—worse than the baseline accuracy of 63.7 without adaptation."

## Note

This is presented as an observed case study of context collapse and supports the claimed risk of monolithic rewriting rather than a universal failure rate.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Zhang et al., Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ACE divides work across three roles: Generator, Reflector, and Curator.

## Source Excerpt

"ACE introduces a structured division of labor across three roles (Figure 4): the Generator, which produces reasoning trajectories; the Reflector, which distills concrete insights from successes and errors; and the Curator, which integrates these insights into structured context updates."

## Note

This is a method-design statement, not a benchmark outcome.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: method
citation: Zhang et al., Section 3.1 Incremental Delta Updates
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ACE represents context as itemized bullets that contain metadata and content rather than as one monolithic prompt.

## Source Excerpt

"A core design principle of ACE is to represent context as a collection of structured, itemized bullets, rather than a single monolithic prompt... [a bullet] consists of (1) metadata, including a unique identifier and counters tracking how often it was marked helpful or harmful; and (2) content, capturing a small unit such as a reusable strategy, domain concept, or common failure mode."

## Note

This describes the internal structure used for adaptation.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: method
citation: Zhang et al., Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ACE merges compact delta entries into existing context with lightweight non-LLM logic, and multiple deltas can be merged in parallel.

## Source Excerpt

"The Curator then synthesizes these lessons into compact delta entries, which are merged deterministically into the existing context by lightweight, non-LLM logic. Because updates are itemized and localized, multiple deltas can be merged in parallel, enabling batched adaptation at scale."

## Note

This is the paper's stated mechanism for scaling updates and avoiding full rewrites.
```

```markdown
---

evidence_id: evidence-09

On AppWorld offline adaptation with ground-truth labels, ReAct + ACE reached 59.4 average accuracy versus 46.4 for ReAct + GEPA, 46.0 for ReAct + ICL, and 42.4 for ReAct baseline.

## Source Excerpt

"ReAct 63.7 42.9 41.5 21.6 42.4" and "ReAct + GEPA ✓ ... 46.4 + 4.0" and "ReAct + ICL ✓ ... 46.0 + 3.6" and "ReAct + ACE ✓ ... 59.4 + 17.0"

## Note

The average metric is the rightmost AppWorld average reported in Table 1.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-035
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: claim-support
citation: Zhang et al., Table 2 and Section 4.4 Analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper reports that when reliable feedback signals are absent, adaptive methods can degrade; in Table 2, online ACE without GT labels scored 72.9 average versus 76.6 with GT labels, and DC without GT labels scored 65.4 versus 71.8 with GT labels.

## Source Excerpt

"However, we also observe that in the absence of reliable feedback signals (e.g., ground-truth labels or execution outcomes), both ACE and other adaptive methods such as Dynamic Cheatsheet may degrade" and "DC (CU) ✓ ... 71.8 + 2.7" / "DC (CU) ✗ ... 65.4 − 3.7" / "ACE ✓ ... 76.6 + 7.5" / "ACE ✗ ... 72.9 + 3.8"

## Note

This is a limitation claim tied to the quality of feedback, and the reported degradation depends on the specific benchmark/task setting.
```
